ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for stir-fried seasonal mixed vegetables; closest parent 'stir-fried vegetable food product' (FOODON_03301710) used as the nearest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Vegetables and vegetable products — cooked vegetables
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (cooked vegetables) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code for Chinese-style stir-fried seasonal mixed vegetables exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 'Stir-fried vegetables' is the closest available term; seasonal specificity is not captured.

main_ingredients: seasonal vegetables (e.g. chye sim, kai lan, bok choy, broccoli, snap peas, carrots)
oyster sauce or light soy sauce

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of stir-fried seasonal vegetables in Singapore is approximately 200 g, presented as a shared side dish or individual accompaniment to rice.

nutrient_source.title: Stir-fried mixed vegetables — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central composite estimate for Chinese-style stir-fried vegetables
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exactly matches 'stir-fried seasonal vegetables' as a generic dish. Values are adapted from HPB Foodo entries for stir-fried leafy vegetables (chye sim, kai lan) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central item 'Vegetables, mixed, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt' (FDC ID 170073) and USDA SR Legacy 'Vegetables, stir-fried, Chinese restaurant'. Macros represent a weighted composite typical of Chinese restaurant-style stir-fried seasonal vegetables with light sauce and oil. Source match type is 'adapted'.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; mixed cooked vegetables analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chinese-style stir-fried seasonal mixed vegetables. GI of 32 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed cooked non-starchy vegetables (GI range 15–40, representative value ~32, low category). Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (32 × 10.4) / 100 ≈ 3.3. Estimation basis: Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC et al., 'International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021', American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: A 200 g restaurant serving contributes approximately 620 mg sodium, which represents about 27% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-sauce preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high dietary fibre content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 32 and 2.3 g dietary fibre per 100 g, stir-fried seasonal vegetables produce a minimal glycaemic response and are suitable as a low-GL side dish for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided sauce quantity is controlled to limit added sugars and sodium.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high dietary fibre, and antioxidant micronutrients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Stir-fried seasonal vegetables are low in saturated fat (0.5 g per 100 g) and provide dietary fibre, vitamin C, and vitamin A, which support cardiovascular health; the primary concern is sodium from cooking sauces, and patients with CVD should request light-sauce preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried seasonal vegetables (清炒时蔬, qīng chǎo shí shū) is a classic Chinese and Singaporean side dish consisting of fresh seasonal leafy or mixed vegetables — commonly chye sim (菜心), kai lan (芥兰), bok choy (白菜), broccoli, snap peas, or carrots — cooked rapidly in a hot wok with garlic, vegetable oil, and a light seasoning of oyster sauce or soy sauce. The high-heat wok technique preserves the vegetables' vibrant colour, crisp-tender texture, and nutritional integrity. The dish is mildly savoury with a subtle umami note from the sauce, and a slight char aroma from wok hei. It is a staple accompaniment to rice or noodle dishes across Chinese restaurants, hawker centres, and zi char stalls in Singapore. Nutritionally, it is low in calories (approximately 62 kcal per 100 g), low in fat, and provides meaningful dietary fibre (2.3 g/100 g), vitamin C (28 mg/100 g), vitamin A (95 mcg RAE/100 g), and folate (55 mcg DFE/100 g). The glycemic index is low (approximately 32), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The primary nutritional concern is sodium from cooking sauces, which can reach 310 mg per 100 g in restaurant preparations. The dish is vegetarian-adaptable (when oyster sauce is omitted) and is generally halal-compliant. It is widely available across Chinese restaurants and hawker stalls in Singapore, often listed as 'seasonal vegetables', 'market vegetables', or '时蔬' on menus.
common_user_queries: How many calories in stir-fried seasonal vegetables?
What vegetables are used in Chinese stir-fry?
Is 清炒时蔬 suitable for weight loss?
How much sodium is in stir-fried vegetables from a Chinese restaurant?
What is the GI of stir-fried vegetables?

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exactly matches 'stir-fried seasonal vegetables' as a generic Chinese restaurant dish. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo entries for stir-fried leafy vegetables and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central mixed cooked vegetable data. GI is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for mixed cooked non-starchy vegetables. Confidence is medium because the dish composition varies by restaurant and season.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input value 'Grains and staples' to 'Vegetables', which accurately reflects the nature of this dish. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero: stir-fried vegetables prepared with vegetable oil contain no industrially produced trans fats; confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients contributing cholesterol in standard preparation. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; B12 is naturally zero. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: vegetables do not naturally contain meaningful vitamin D; confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g (1.8), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.2), potassium_mg (280.0), calcium_mg (72.0), iron_mg (1.2), vitamin_c_mg (28.0), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (95.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.07), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.09), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (0.8), vitamin_b6_mg (0.12), folate_mcg_dfe (55.0), vitamin_e_mg (1.1), phosphorus_mg (58.0), magnesium_mg (22.0), zinc_mg (0.4), selenium_mcg (1.2), water_g (85.5) are adapted estimates from HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central composite for stir-fried mixed vegetables. 7. Sodium value of 310 mg/100 g reflects typical restaurant preparation with light soy or oyster sauce; home-prepared versions may be significantly lower. 8. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for mixed cooked non-starchy vegetables; no direct measurement for this specific dish exists. 9. Dietary flags: is_vegan set to false because standard restaurant preparation uses oyster sauce; vegetarian flag set to true as the base dish is plant-based. 10. Aliases consistency verified: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases list.
